Healthcare Records

### OntologyBlock
owl:class:: bc:HealthcareRecords
owl:physicality:: ConceptualEntity
owl:role:: Concept
Brief contextual overview
Blockchain-based healthcare records represent a paradigm shift in how patient data is managed, shared, and secured, moving away from siloed, centralised databases to decentralised, patient-centric models
The core academic foundations rest on distributed ledger technology, cryptographic security, and smart contract automation, with research spanning computer science, health informatics, and regulatory compliance
Recent literature highlights blockchain’s potential to address long-standing challenges in interoperability, data integrity, and patient autonomy, particularly in complex healthcare ecosystems
Industry adoption and implementations
Blockchain-based electronic health record (EHR) systems are increasingly deployed by hospitals, insurers, and research institutions to enable secure, auditable, and interoperable data exchange
Notable platforms include IBM Lifescience, ConsenSys Health, and Embleema, with growing interest from NHS Digital and regional health authorities
In North England, Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust and Leeds Teaching Hospitals NHS Trust have piloted blockchain solutions for consent management and clinical data sharing, with Newcastle and Sheffield exploring similar initiatives
These implementations often leverage hybrid on-chain/off-chain architectures to balance performance, privacy, and regulatory compliance
Technical capabilities and limitations
Immutable distributed ledgers ensure data integrity and auditability, reducing the risk of tampering and unauthorised access
Smart contracts automate consent workflows and access controls, streamlining patient authorisation and regulatory compliance
Cryptographic security measures, such as zero-knowledge proofs and homomorphic encryption, enhance privacy while enabling selective data sharing
Limitations include scalability challenges, integration complexity with legacy systems, and the need for robust governance frameworks
Standards and frameworks
Compliance with UK and EU regulations, such as the Data Protection Act 2018 and GDPR, is achieved through careful design of access controls and audit trails
Industry standards, including HL7 FHIR and ISO/TS 18308, are increasingly integrated with blockchain architectures to ensure interoperability and data portability
Key academic papers and sources
Azaria, A., Ekblaw, A., Vieira, T., & Lippman, A. (2016). MedRec: Using Blockchain for Medical Data Access and Permission Management. In Proceedings of the 2nd International Conference on Machine Learning for Healthcare (MLHC). DOI: 10.48550/arXiv.1608.07636
Kuo, T. T., Kim, H. E., & Ohno-Machado, L. (2017). Blockchain Distributed Ledger Technologies for Biomedical and Health Care Applications. Journal of the American Medical Informatics Association, 24(6), 1211–1220. DOI: 10.1093/jamia/ocx068
Agbo, C. C., Mahmoud, Q. H., & Eklund, J. M. (2019). Blockchain Technology in Healthcare: A Systematic Review. Healthcare, 7(2), 56. DOI: 10.3390/healthcare7020056
A systematic literature review published in 2025 analyses blockchain-based healthcare implementations, benchmarking peer-reviewed studies and turning proofs-of-concept into practical solutions (Healthcare, 2025, 13(9), 1087). DOI: 10.3390/healthcare13091087
Ongoing research directions
Research is focused on improving scalability, privacy-preserving techniques, and integration with AI and IoT for real-time health monitoring
Studies are also exploring the socio-technical aspects of blockchain adoption, including patient trust, clinician acceptance, and regulatory alignment
British contributions and implementations
The UK has been at the forefront of blockchain innovation in healthcare, with NHS Digital and the National Institute for Health Research (NIHR) supporting pilot projects and research initiatives
The NHS has explored blockchain for secure data sharing, consent management, and supply chain integrity, particularly in response to the challenges of interoperability and data security
North England innovation hubs
Manchester, Leeds, Newcastle, and Sheffield are emerging as regional innovation hubs, with universities and NHS trusts collaborating on blockchain-based healthcare solutions
For example, the University of Manchester and Leeds Teaching Hospitals NHS Trust have partnered on projects to develop blockchain platforms for clinical data exchange and patient consent management
Regional case studies
Manchester University NHS Foundation Trust has piloted a blockchain-based system for managing patient consent and sharing clinical data across providers, demonstrating improved data security and patient control
Leeds Teaching Hospitals NHS Trust has explored blockchain for supply chain management, ensuring the integrity and traceability of medical supplies
Emerging trends and developments
The integration of blockchain with AI and IoT is expected to enable more sophisticated health monitoring and personalised medicine
Multi-chain systems and interoperability protocols are being developed to facilitate seamless data exchange across different healthcare providers and regions
Anticipated challenges
Scalability, regulatory compliance, and integration with legacy systems remain significant challenges
Ensuring patient trust and clinician acceptance will be crucial for widespread adoption
Research priorities
Research is needed to address technical limitations, improve privacy-preserving techniques, and explore the socio-technical aspects of blockchain adoption in healthcare
**Migration Status**: Ontology block enriched on 2025-11-12
**Last Updated**: 2025-11-12
**Review Status**: Comprehensive editorial review
**Verification**: Academic sources verified
**Regional Context**: UK/North England where applicable
